content.problemStatement:
Devu is learning Combinatorics in his college. He find it very interesting to calculate number of ways of going to point **(c,d)** from point **(a,b)** in co-ordinate plane. We can take horizontal and vertical steps only and can not visit at a point twice. In a step, you can move one unit only. We have to reach to the point **(c,d)** from the point **(a,b)** using **abs(a-c)+ abs(b-d)** steps only.


 
Devu has two sets of points. Set **A** contains points having **X** co-ordinate **0** and **Y** co-ordinates varying from **1** to **N**(both inclusive). Set **B** contains points having **X** co-ordinate **K** and **Y** co-ordinates varying from **1** to **N**(both inclusive).  Both sets contains **N** number of integral points. He wants to calculate the sum of number of ways to going to the each point of set **B** from the each point of set **A** .



As the answer can be large, print it modulo **1000000007**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers N and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer representing the sum of the number of ways to go to each point of set B from each point of set A.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first sample case, the number of ways to go to each point of set B from each point of set A is calculated as follows: ways[(0,1)->(2,1)]= 1, ways[(0,2)->(2,2)]= 1, ways[(0,1)->(2,2)]= 3, ways[(0,2)->(2,1)]= 3. So, the total number of ways = 8.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of combinatorics. We need to calculate the number of ways to go to each point of set B from each point of set A. This can be done using the binomial coefficient formula. We will calculate the binomial coefficient for each pair of points and sum them up. To handle large values, we will use modular arithmetic.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * log(K)) — explain: per query is O(N * log(K)), total is O(T * N * log(K))
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — explain: we only use a constant amount of space to store the intermediate results
